title: Give Me Your Tired: The Primary Source Record of Who Made Them Tired in the First Place
teaser: Emma Lazarus wrote the promise in 1883. The CIA wrote the policy memos in 1954. The IMF wrote the structural adjustment conditions in the 1980s. The State Department co-signed the 2009 coup. The primary source record of American immigration contains all of these documents. The current political debate cites only the first one.
verifiedSource: Library of Congress; CIA Declassified Archives (FOIA); State Department Historical Records; NAFTA Implementation Records; Congressional Research Service; UN Human Development Reports
body:
The inscription on the base of the Statue of Liberty reads, in part: "Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free." Emma Lazarus wrote those words in 1883. They were cast in bronze and mounted on the pedestal in 1903. They remain there today, facing the Atlantic, technically welcoming anyone who can read them from the water.

This article is about the other documents. The ones filed in Langley, Virginia. In the offices of the International Monetary Fund. In the State Department cables released under the Freedom of Information Act. The primary source record of American immigration policy does not begin at the border. It begins with the policy that preceded the migrant by decades. To debate immigration without reading both sets of documents is to argue about a fever without consulting the chart that documents how the patient got sick.

The Cargo Truck Is Not the Beginning of the Story

When human beings are found dead in the back of an unventilated cargo truck in Texas, the news cycle asks how they got across the border. The prior question is why a person with a family, a language, a community, and a life would choose a cargo truck as a rational option. That question has a documented answer, and it does not begin with the individual. It begins with the policy environment that eliminated every less dangerous alternative.

People do not abandon their countries on impulse. The research record on forced migration is consistent: the overwhelming majority of people in forced migration are fleeing conditions -- violence, state collapse, economic destruction -- not seeking adventure. The question of who produced those conditions is answerable. The answers are in the primary source record.

Guatemala, 1954: The Document That Started a Migration Pattern

On June 27, 1954, the democratically elected President of Guatemala, Jacobo Arbenz, was removed from power in a CIA-backed coup code-named Operation PBSUCCESS. The stated rationale was communist influence. The documented context was that Arbenz had enacted land reform affecting the United Fruit Company, an American corporation, which had lobbied the Eisenhower administration aggressively. The CIA organized and funded the paramilitary operation that ended Guatemalan democracy. What followed was four decades of military dictatorship and civil war that killed an estimated 200,000 people, the majority of them Indigenous Maya civilians. Guatemala is currently one of the top ten countries of origin for migrants apprehended at the United States southern border. The displacement did not begin at the Rio Grande. It began in a policy decision documented in files that are now publicly available in the CIA reading room.

Honduras, 2009: The Coup the State Department Watched

On June 28, 2009, Honduran President Manuel Zelaya was removed from power by the military and flown out of the country in his pajamas. The Obama State Department, under Secretary Hillary Clinton, declined to formally designate the removal as a coup, a distinction with significant legal consequences: a formal coup designation would have triggered automatic suspension of all U.S. aid to Honduras. The U.S. subsequently recognized the post-coup government. Honduras, in the decade that followed, became one of the most violent countries in the Western Hemisphere. San Pedro Sula recorded one of the highest murder rates of any city on earth not formally at war. The Honduran migrant caravans that became a fixture of American political debate in 2018 and 2019 originated in the conditions that followed 2009. The policy record is in the State Department cables. The National Security Archive has published them.

NAFTA and the Farmer Who Could Not Compete With a Subsidy

When the North American Free Trade Agreement took effect in 1994, it removed Mexico's agricultural tariff protections. American corn, produced by heavily subsidized industrial farming operations, entered the Mexican market at prices below what Mexican smallholder farmers could produce corn for. Approximately 1.5 million Mexican farmers were displaced from agriculture in the decade following NAFTA's implementation. The trade agreement that was sold as a prosperity mechanism for Mexico produced, in documentable sequence, the rural displacement that drove the Mexican migration wave that followed. The policy architects are on record. The Carnegie Endowment, the Economic Policy Institute, and the Congressional Research Service have all published analysis of this sequence. It is not contested.

The Demand Side of the Cartel Economy

The cartels that currently make the northern triangle of Central America -- Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador -- ungovernable enough to drive mass emigration are financed primarily by drug revenue. The United States is the world's largest consumer market for illegal narcotics. RAND Corporation has documented the direct financial relationship between American drug consumption and cartel revenue in Mexico and Central America. The United States is simultaneously the destination of migrants fleeing cartel violence and the primary revenue source sustaining the cartels they are fleeing. This is not an argument. It is an accounting identity. The ledger closes on itself.

The Mirror a Great Nation Can Afford to Look Into

None of this is an argument that America is uniquely villainous among nations. The historical record of great powers shaping smaller nations' political economies in self-interested ways is not a uniquely American story. It is, however, an American story, and the specificity matters when the policy debate is specifically about America's border.

The United States is one of the most consequential nations in the history of human civilization. That consequence runs in both directions. The Marshall Plan rebuilt Europe. Operation PBSUCCESS destabilized Guatemala. The GI Bill created the American middle class. NAFTA's agricultural provisions helped dismantle Mexico's. The nation that built the interstate highway system also backed the paramilitary force that killed 200,000 Guatemalans. Both things are in the primary source record.

A country with this level of documented consequence in the political and economic conditions of its neighbors cannot debate immigration as if the migrants materialized without cause. The cargo truck did not come from nowhere. The Statue of Liberty inscription does not say "Give me your tired, your poor -- provided we had no role in making them tired, and provided they arrive through channels we have not systematically closed."

The promise is still on the pedestal. The policy record is in the archives. A nation great enough to have shaped the world is great enough to read both documents in the same sitting. That is not criticism. It is the minimum threshold of the accountability that greatness requires.
